title: Tunisian tajine
subtitle: Tunisian baked spiced egg and meat cake
excerpt: Minced meat or chicken mixed with eggs, spices, potato, and cheese, then baked into a thick, sliceable set cake. Nothing like the Moroccan tagine it shares a name with, the Tunisian tajine is a baked egg dish closer to a frittata, cut into squares and served warm or at room temperature.
sourceNotes: The Tunisian tajine (or tajiine) is the baked egg dish that shares its name with the Moroccan tagine pot but is an entirely different preparation. It combines eggs, cooked meat, vegetables, and cheese in a thick baked cake. Variations include chicken (as here), minced lamb, potato, harissa, and various cheeses. It is made for celebrations and picnics.

content.text: The key technique is cooking the filling ingredients before combining with the eggs. Raw ingredients release moisture and prevent the tajine from setting. Cook the onion and chicken completely, drain well, and cool slightly before mixing with the beaten eggs.

Use a deep baking dish or oven-safe frying pan, the tajine needs to be at least 4 cm deep to set properly in the centre. It is done when a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean and the top is golden. Rest for 10 minutes before cutting.

Heat the oven to 180°C. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a frying pan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 5 minutes. Add the {{garlic}}, {{cumin-ground}}, and {{turmeric}} and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{chicken-breast}} and cook for 8 to 10 minutes until cooked through. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper}}. Remove from the heat and cool for 5 minutes. In a large bowl, beat the {{eggs}} well. Add the cooked chicken and onion mixture, the cubed {{potato}}, {{parsley-flat}}, and half the {{gruyere}}. Mix thoroughly. Pour into a greased 25 cm square baking dish or similar. Scatter the remaining gruyere on top. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until set through and golden on top. A skewer inserted in the centre should come out clean. Rest for 10 minutes, then cut into squares. Serve warm or at room temperature.

items.fix: Ensure the cooked chicken and onion are well-drained before adding to the egg. Continue baking and check at 5-minute intervals. The tajine should be completely firm when pressed in the centre
items.cause: Filling too wet or oven too cool
items.symptom: Tajine not setting in the centre

items.fix: Cover with foil and continue baking. The foil prevents further browning while the centre continues to cook
items.cause: Oven too hot or dish too shallow
items.symptom: Top browning before centre is set

items.fix: Do not overbake. Remove as soon as the skewer comes out clean, the residual heat continues cooking after the oven is off
items.cause: Eggs overcooked or too many eggs for the filling ratio

content.text: The Tunisian tajine has nothing to do with the Moroccan tagine beyond sharing an Arabic root word. In Tunisia it is a baked egg dish made for celebrations, packed lunches, and picnics. It travels well and is just as good cold as warm. The name can confuse visitors expecting the Moroccan braised dish. Other versions of Tunisian tajine use minced lamb instead of chicken, spinach, tuna, or a purely vegetable filling. It is cut into squares and eaten with harissa and bread, or as part of a larger spread.
